title: Database Tools & GUIs
intro: You've just joined a team and need to look at the production database to debug an issue. Similar to how you can read a book at the library, on an e-reader, or by audiobook, there are different tools to access the same data. But you've never connected to a remote database before. Let's walk through the process and learn about the tools developers use.

teach.title: Database Tools = Different Ways to Read a Book
teach.body: You can read a book by going to the library (command line), using an e-reader (database GUI), or having someone read it to you (ORM in your code). All three get you the same content, just in different ways. Database tools are the same -- different interfaces to the same data.

teach.title: The Car Dashboard
teach.body: Database tools are like your car's dashboard. You could pop the hood and check the engine directly (command line), use the dashboard gauges and warning lights (GUI tool), or let your car's computer handle everything automatically (ORM in code). Most drivers glance at the dashboard daily but only open the hood when something needs deeper inspection. Developers do the same -- GUI for quick checks, command line for precision work.

teach.title: Three Ways to Access a Database
teach.body:
Developers typically use one of these:

1. **Command line** (`psql` for PostgreSQL, `sqlite3` for SQLite) -- fast, powerful, but text-only
2. **GUI tools** (TablePlus, DBeaver, pgAdmin) -- visual, great for exploring data
3. **From your code** (using an ORM or query builder) -- how your app accesses the database

Most developers use a GUI for exploring and the command line or code for making changes.

teach.title: Connection Strings
teach.body:
To connect to a database, you need a **connection string** -- an address that tells your tool where the database is:

`postgresql://username:password@hostname:5432/database_name`

This includes: the database type, your login credentials, the server address, the port number, and which database to open. This string is usually stored in an environment variable for security.

Knowing your database tools turns you from someone who builds apps into someone who can debug and fix them when real users report problems. That ability to keep a live app running smoothly is what separates vibecoded side projects from vibecoded businesses that earn revenue.

You can access a database through the command line, a visual GUI tool, or from your application code. Connection strings tell your tools where the database lives. Never hardcode connection strings in your source code -- use environment variables.

start.narrative: Your team lead says: 'A user reports they can't see their orders. Check the database to see if their data exists. The connection info is in the .env file.' What's your first step?

choices.text: Check the .env file for the database connection string
Open the production source code and grep for the database URL
Ask the team lead to paste the password in chat

found-env.narrative: You find: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://app_user:secret123@db.myapp.com:5432/production. You also notice the app has a SEPARATE staging database listed below it. Before connecting, what do you do?

choices.text: Use the staging connection string to investigate -- production reads are still risky and staging usually has the same schema
Use production -- the bug is in production data, only production data will reproduce it
Use production but plan to be fast and careful

wrong-source.narrative: Connection strings should NEVER be in source code -- they contain passwords. The standard practice is to store them in .env files (which are not committed to git) or in environment variables set on the server. Check the .env file instead.

choices.text: Right, check the .env file for the connection string

partial-ask.narrative: That works, but the connection info should already be in the .env file -- that's the standard place teams store database credentials. Check there first so you don't need to bother your lead.

choices.text: Let me check the .env file first

pick-tool.narrative: Now choose how to connect. You need to look at one user's orders -- a quick exploration, not a script.

choices.text: GUI tool like TablePlus -- I can browse tables, click into rows, and write a SELECT in the same window
psql command line -- fast to launch and the query is the same SQL
Write a one-off script in the app codebase that prints the orders

write-query.narrative: You're connected. The reporting user's id is 42. Which query do you run to confirm whether their order data exists?

choices.text: SELECT id, user_id, created_at, status FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10;
SELECT * FROM orders; -- I'll scan visually for user 42
DELETE FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42; -- wait, I meant SELECT

interpret.narrative: Result: zero rows. Meanwhile, SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE id = 42 returns one row -- the user exists. What do you check next to figure out why their orders are missing?

choices.text: Check whether the user_id column on orders matches the users.id type/value, and look for any orders linked to a different account with this user's email
Check whether the orders table is empty for everyone -- rule out a system-wide outage
Tell the user they have no orders and close the ticket

end-great.narrative: You join the tables on email and discover the user recently changed their email and re-registered, creating a new account with user_id 87. Their old orders are linked to user_id 42's email but the new account is 87. The data is there -- it's just linked to their old account. You report the finding so the team can merge the accounts.
end-great.feedback: Great investigation. You used the right tool for an exploratory task (a GUI), connected via the .env config (the right place), wrote a scoped SELECT (not a destructive query), and joined the dots between users and orders. This is what daily database detective work looks like.

end-good.narrative: You confirm other users do have orders, so this is not a system-wide outage -- it's specific to user 42. You hand the ticket back to a backend engineer with this scoping. Useful information, but you stopped one query short of finding the actual cause.
end-good.feedback: Scoping the bug (system-wide vs user-specific) is a smart first move and you did it well. Next time, follow the thread one more step: once you know it's user-specific, ask 'what's different about THIS user?' -- different account, different region, different role -- and a JOIN usually finds the answer.

end-weak.narrative: You close the ticket as 'no orders for this user'. The next morning the user emails screenshots of three order confirmations from last month. Support has to reopen the ticket, escalate it past you, and your team trusts your database work less.
end-weak.feedback: Zero rows from one query is a starting point, not a conclusion. The user reported they couldn't SEE their orders -- not that they had never placed any. The whole point of having database tools is to dig past the first answer until the data tells a coherent story.
